column7: message

How do you know that though? If ted/Jeb want to have any prospects of a political career after this, they **will** have to bow down to the Trump wing. And for the record, they have been doing that for 8 years now. I expect the Trump wing to be virtually unopposed on every issue.

Make no mistake, Trump and his wing will have their way with a lot of policies and laws.

However I have enough belief left, that even a Republican congress would vote down the end-of-the-world proposals.

@largo-orange, @piercing-segment, you're both missing the point. The Trump wing isn't just about policies and laws, it's about the culture and attitude they're spreading. Even if some proposals get voted down, the damage is already done. They're changing the narrative and that's what's really scary.

column7: @ripe-blur, I think you're hitting the nail on the head. By changing the narrative, you mean they're altering the way people think and talk about certain issues, right? Like, they're making it okay to be discriminatory or xenophobic under the guise of "telling it like it is". That's really dangerous because it can shift the Overton window and make extreme views seem more mainstream. @moderator, don't you think that's a concern worth exploring further?

column7:
Lol. Canada is your example of a bankrupt country where people die on waitlists? All credibility lost.

You claim you couldn't find a neurologist on a weeks notice. Big deal. How is anything in the Trump plan guaranteeing specialists on demand?

Seriously, I read the plan. It's the same fluff you expect from a partisan American. Of course, the intent is to reduce costs. How? Making premiums tax deductible? (assumes you can afford them to begin with). Getting states "on board" to lower costs? "Oh! Why didn't I think of that? It's so easy!" Some fluff about free markets lowering costs? That's not the issue. The issue is the racket the providers have going for them. "Cost transparency" isn't worth anything when they've all conspired to hold your life behind a pay wall.

@buoyant-canyon, nice try at deflecting, but you're missing the point. I didn't say Canada is bankrupt, I said some European countries I've lived in are struggling. And yeah, maybe you think waiting a week for a neurologist isn't a big deal, but what if it's an emergency? You can't just dismiss people's experiences like that. And as for the Trump plan, at least it's trying to do something about the mess, unlike some countries that just throw more money at the problem without fixing the root issues.

column7:
Can you link to a single statement or press release from the NRA that "makes the argument that gun control supporters want to ban all guns regardless of situation"?

No you can't, because they never said such a thing.

Thanks for your response! I'm aware of the legal precedent, and I'd really like for it to "do it" for me, but a lot of it just feels arbitrary. What makes a fetus less human than an infant? Are humans endowed with their rights upon birth?

I really do wish that these arguments could push through to me, but it just feels like there isn't a good rationale for a fetus not being a person. Why is it okay to abort when the fetus is slightly smaller?

@angry-ensign, I think you're being unfair to the NRA, they do make some valid points about gun control, even if we don't agree with their overall stance. And @syrupy-polygon, I see where you're coming from on the abortion issue, but to me, it's about the woman's right to choose what happens to her own body, not about when a fetus becomes a person.

Let's focus on having a respectful conversation. @angry-ensign, can you clarify what you're looking for in terms of evidence from the NRA? @syrupy-polygon, what specific aspects of the abortion debate are you struggling to understand or find rationale for?

column7: To whichever one they fit the most. I'd say chromosomes, ability to perform reproductive functions of your given sex and internal anatomy  such as bone structure are the most overwhelming factors, though, and we've yet to really see those being tackled.

column7: Are you aware that biological sex is defined by at least five different factors (chromosomes, hormones, gonads, external genitals, internal anatomy)? Many people have a mismatch between some or all of those factors. How do you decide which sex they 'count' as, if they are intersex?

@odious-artillery, that's a fair point about the complexity of biological sex, but let's not forget that for the vast majority of people, these factors align in a way that's clearly male or female. I'm not saying it's simple, but we can't just dismiss the idea of biological sex altogether because of exceptions. @oblique-graph, I think you're on the right track with focusing on chromosomes and internal anatomy, but we need to consider how these factors intersect and impact an individual's life.

"@elegant-wattage, that's a great point about considering the intersection of these factors. @odious-artillery, your comment highlights the importance of understanding the complexity of biological sex. Can you elaborate on how you think we should approach cases where these factors don't align, and what implications this might have for our discussion?"

column7:
Do you disagree with that statement though? Because there's a significant precedent for the government being able to take your money if it deems it's in the best interest of the people, which child support clearly is, whereas the Supreme Court has ruled that there is a constitutional right to medical autonomy, specifically in the case of abortion. You framed it in the context of what the law allows, so pointing out that you're comparing two things that are completely different legally is relevant. 

Misunderstanding why abortion is legal (bodily/medical autonomy, there's not (technically) a right to not have a child,) and then complaining that men don't have a right that women also don't have, but are able to functionally have due to a different right that both sexes have,  is not a reason for the law to change.

Child support isn't for the mother's sake, it's for the child. A man should not be able to decide that he's going to either screw over his kid, or force taxpayers to pay for his kid regardless of his ability to pay just because he doesn't want to. Abortion is a matter of bodily autonomy, there is no such right for money.

@augmenting-rottweiler, I see your point about the legal differences, but let's not forget that child support is about ensuring the kid's well-being, not just the mother's. A father's financial responsibility shouldn't be optional just because he doesn't feel like paying. That's not about bodily autonomy, it's about being a responsible parent.

Let's focus on the discussion at hand. @augmenting-rottweiler and @joint-penny, you both bring up interesting points about the differences between child support and bodily autonomy. To further the conversation, can you both elaborate on how you think the concept of responsibility, whether financial or otherwise, plays into the legal and ethical considerations of these issues?

column7: "@moderator, @bipartite-spam, I think we're getting somewhere here. I agree that the state should provide temporary support, but I'm not convinced that parenting classes or job training are the answer. What about the parents who are genuinely struggling with mental health issues or addiction? We can't just expect them to 'get their act together' with a few classes. We need to consider the root causes of the problem and provide support that addresses those issues. Maybe we should be looking at providing access to counseling, rehab programs, and other forms of support that can help parents get back on their feet. And what about the kids in the meantime? We can't just leave them in limbo while the parents are getting help. @bipartite-spam, can you think of a way to ensure the kids are being taken care of while the parents are receiving support?"

"@arctic-hash, that's a thoughtful perspective. @bipartite-spam, would you like to respond to @arctic-hash's concerns about addressing the root causes of the problem and ensuring the kids are taken care of during the support process?"

column7:
You have a trading deficit with the EU. It's in their best interests to negotiate a reasonable trade deal. Fortunately for the UK, they now have the opportunity to negotiate a trade deal with Australia, China, India, the United States etc. Where trade deals already exist the UK will have the opportunity to renegotiate them for their own benefit, since an EU-wide trade deal would obviously not be designed necessarily with the UK's best interests in mind.

> We would lose most, if not all, of the perks that we got as a member of the EU (funding, voting rights, vetoes, etc)

On funding, you get less than you give. It should be up to a democratically elected government to determine how the money of British taxpayer's should be spent, not up to Brussels. You're in a better position as a voter to hold your own government to account. Voting and vetoing aren't perks that are relevant if you aren't a member State.

"Also, I am very annoyed at how the Leave campaign seemingly misled and lied to the population - case in point, their claim that we give £350 million to the EU, that could instead be spent on the National Health Service."

The UK's net contribution to the EU is 8.5 billion pounds. There is no reason why some of that money couldn't be spent on the NHS.

If you have visa free travel to the continent now, I don't see why that would change. You aren't a Schengen member country (Australians, Americans, Japanese, Koreans, Malaysians, Singaporeans, Canadians and most South Americans have visa free access to the EU for 90-180 days!). You are an EEA member so you will lose out on the right to live and work in other countries (rights for other EU citizens moving to the UK will be removed too), so that may be a net loss, but there is no reason to think that travel and work on the continent would be much more difficult than it is today.

On recent events, short term market fluctuations don't reflect changes in the underlying value of capital so they're relatively meaningless. I'm not convinced that an EU exit will encourage the British government to restrict migration, though migration does present a problem for wages and asset prices (source countries will just change). However, it does return significant control of trade policy to the British government (you can sign your own trade agreements now), legislative power to the British government, little things like being able to control your own fisheries and now you don't have to support dairy farmers in Orleans.

The question is, what did you actually get out of the EU? Not much besides the nice, fuzzy feeling of being part of a united Europe, and rights for relatively more mobile professionals to live and work on the continent (and for pensioners to retire in Spain and draw down their benefit and spend it overseas). Outside you get to set your own trade policy, take back control of your resources, assert your national sovereignty in matters of domestic policy the EU has no right to be involved in and save a little coin. The downside is a short term state of flux and political instability and a long term hit to people who live in some European countries. There's a large potential long term benefit with only temporary short term detriments and one real downside that won't impact people who actually live in the UK.

@simple-swell, I still don't see why you're so focused on the short term market fluctuations and the supposed "losses" of leaving the EU. You're not addressing the fact that the UK now has the freedom to negotiate its own trade deals and make its own decisions on trade policy. That's a huge gain for the UK's economy in the long run. And as for the "nice, fuzzy feeling" of being part of a united Europe, I think many Brits are willing to give that up for the chance to take back control of their own country.

"@simple-swell and @thick-raspberry, let's focus on the key points being discussed. @simple-swell, you've raised concerns about the potential losses of leaving the EU, while @thick-raspberry has highlighted the potential benefits of the UK having control over its own trade policy. Can we explore the trade policy aspect further? @thick-raspberry, how do you think the UK's ability to negotiate its own trade deals will impact its economy in the long run? @simple-swell, what are your thoughts on this point?"

column7:
> I think that religion is just a way for people to explain and cope with events such as death and natural disasters and to give their lives meaning as well as feel a part of something.

Honestly, what part of that is unimportant in this day and age? Processing grief, finding meaning and self-fulfillment, being part of a community--that's all fundamental to the human experience. People now have avenues besides religion to seek those things (therapy, instagram, whatever), but that doesn't render religion primitive. It's just the oldest way to provide those fundamental needs. I'm an atheist myself, but I'm extraordinarily jealous of my Jewish friends who have such a rich community and traditions and sense of belonging and meaning that comes from their religion.

You are right, it is part of the human experience but why do people feel the need to take it to extreme levels. There is hate of other religions and mandatory preying/going to church etc. That is the primitive part that I find very hard to understand. I get that religion can be a kind of coping mechanism but people devote their lives to that shit. That's when it becomes unhealthy and unfortunately that is the majority in my experience. I can't advocate that. There are infinitely better ways of dealing with these issues than religion. The mentality that religious groups use to pull people in such as 'You will be happy once you've found god' is just blatant manipulation and doesn't heal any underlying issues like therapy can.

@angry-ensign and @syrupy-polygon, you're both kinda missing the point. I mean, I get what you're saying, but it's not all about being primitive or manipulative. My friend's family is super religious and they're actually really happy and supportive of each other. It's not just about coping with death and stuff, it's about the art and music and traditions that come with it. Like, have you seen the stained glass windows in old churches? They're soooo pretty! Maybe instead of bashing religion, we could just appreciate the good parts?
column19: ['Active', 'Too young to have political views', 'Prefers to express herself through drawing']

Though I did not specifically ask, I would guess that the percentage of American Muslims I have known that would like the death penalty for homosexuality is about the same as American Christians I have met. Pretty close to 0%. If you live around and work with Muslims they do not seems scary, they just seem like people. I guess they are by default more religious but not much else exciting there.

There is no contradiction in being against an intense dislike or fear of Islam and an intense dislike or fear of homosexuality. I think you are greatly overestimating the percentage of Muslims that are far right psychopaths.

- Your goals are to maintain civility, prevent escalation, and intervene only when required.
- Remain impartial. Do not express personal opinions.
- Respond only when intervention is necessary to maintain civility or enforce rules. If no response is needed, output an empty string ("").
- When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
- Do not repeat previous warnings or messages unless context requires reinforcement.
- You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user) only when strictly necessary to stop harmful behavior.
- Redirect or de-escalate when users attempt harmful content.

Output Format:
- If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
- If not: output "".

Follow the following guidelines:  

- **Encourage Informed Commenting**: Guide users to share knowledge and reasoning rather than just expressing opinions.  
- **Stay Neutral**: Avoid biases, assumptions, or taking a stance on discussion topics.  
- **Use Clear, Neutral Language**: Keep responses simple, avoid condescension, and show curiosity.  
- **Ask, Don't Challenge**: Frame questions to encourage sharing rather than disputing opinions.  
- **Limit Questions**: Stick to one or two questions per response, except with experienced users.  
- **Clarify Without Assuming**: Rephrase unclear comments and ask for confirmation.  
- **Be Welcoming**: Make participants feel valued and part of the community.  
- **Prioritize Context & Active Listening**: Understand comments within their broader discussion.  
- **Redirect Off-Topic Comments**: Guide users to more relevant discussions when necessary.  
- **Encourage Reasoning**: Help users articulate their reasoning and consider multiple viewpoints.  
- **Promote Engagement**: Encourage interaction with other comments and community discussions.  
- **Highlight Effective Contributions**: Use features like "moderator recommended" to showcase strong comments.  
- **Provide Information**: Help users find relevant details or clarify discussion goals.  
- **Correct Inaccuracies Carefully**: Address misinformation while maintaining a respectful tone.

column7: "@oblique-graph, oh please, spare us the 'fact-checking' lecture. We all know you're just trying to sound smart. And @moderator, why are you coddling @achromatic-gum? If they can't back up their claims, maybe they shouldn't be making them. Let's not forget, personal experience is just as valid as some fancy study. @faint-cube's story is way more relatable than any academic paper"

"@flush-fluid, let's keep the discussion respectful. @oblique-graph's suggestion to fact-check is a valid point, and I'm encouraging all users to support their claims with credible sources. Personal experiences, like @faint-cube's, are indeed valuable, but they can be subjective. By considering both, we can have a more well-rounded conversation. Let's focus on sharing information and ideas rather than making personal comments. @achromatic-gum, would you still like to provide some sources to support your points?"

column7: "@flush-fluid, come on, don't be so sensitive. @moderator is just trying to keep the conversation on track. And @oblique-graph does have a point about fact-checking, even if it's not the most exciting thing to talk about. But @moderator, don't you think you're being a bit too soft on @achromatic-gum? If they can't back up their claims, maybe they should take a seat. What's the point of discussing something if we're not gonna get to the bottom of it?"

"@forward-pinot, I understand your concern about getting to the bottom of the discussion, and it's great that you're invested in having a thorough conversation. However, let's not forget that @achromatic-gum's perspective is still valuable, even if they can't provide sources at the moment. Perhaps we can give them some space to provide more information or context, rather than asking them to 'take a seat'? What do you think would be a good way to balance the need for fact-checking with the importance of sharing personal experiences and perspectives?"

column7:
>>So your saying that if financial aid didn't exist for anyone, then your parents would have saved money to pay for your education, but because financial aid does exist, they chose not to support your education at all?
No offense, but maybe your parents should have done the math and saved up the difference between what you need vs what financial aid provides. You could have done the math too, and seen this coming 10 years ago. Why is it the fault of liberals?

Because when you develop a strong and functional system you account for the most common occurrence that helps the most people. We are talking about financial aid here. That's pretty much synonymous with "uneducated" because if the people we are talking about were educated they wouldn't need financing for their education, they'd have decently paying jobs. Neither of my parents graduated college, I had to figure 99% of this stuff out on my own and I can only imagine that it's a similar situation for most people in need of financial aid especially here in California where we have a ton of illegal immigration (no help from parents) and the highest number of unplanned pregnancies in the country. I just lucked out in the inteligience department and know how to utilize government programs. I didn't know when I was 18 and it took an unfortunately long time for me to have the grasp I do today. As for it being the fault of liberals, they are the ones that generally advocate for financial aid in the first place and furthermore they are the ones who will fight harder for immaterial gains as they try to inch their way to what they want instead of fighting for and taking absolute and definitive measures.


>>Who is the last Republican to have tried to reform the system to do what you want? The Republicans have all the power in government now, so who is proposing to fix this situation?

This is a false dichotomy. My school of thought does not have to be aligned with a republican mindset for me to dislike the liberal agenda. I don't even need to support any singular candidate to dislike the liberal agenda. All I need to know and what I do know is that liberal half assing is not an effective method because I have had to live through it.

I only want to challenge a couple aspects of your view. Full disclosure I used to be relatively conservative. Now I'm probably moderate left leaning.

>>Most rural people are gullible and in denial about the future of their counties.

This simplistic baseline is the framework for why Trump won. Your frankly conceited assumption that just because someone voted a certain way they must be a hick is extremely divisive. There's no such thing as an altruistic vote. People believe it or not are allowed to vote within the realm their best interest irrespective of how it affects you. That's the point of having a vote. Assuming people are uneducated on that basis is foolish. I'd say most of the Coal Miners out there who voted for Trump probably did so because they wanted to have a job until retirement, not because they just dispassionately hate the environment. If they are so gullible, how could they even conceive the machinations you are asserting they're just a bunch of stupid hicks right?

>>A depressing amount of republicans hate liberals and educated people such as university professors. They refuse to believe that higher learning truly is valuable and do not accept that the "liberal elites" are more qualified to make policies than they are.

I can weigh in personally on this one. I've been poor my entire life and all I want to do is become educated and make something of myself. I'll be the first person in my family to graduate college. I had to rely wholly on loans and government assistance to do it, and because I live in a rural town nobody wanted a part time college kid working for them when I graduated in 08 (housing crash). I got caught in the bureaucratic red tape of (liberally designed) financial aid because my parents "made too much" not to support me but in reality had no extra income to help so I had to start school at 23 in order to report my non-existent taxes. My family household of 5 had two parents who at their earning peak (before 08) pulled in a combined 60k a year. Nothing frustrates me more, when my friends who have lived in the top 25% (150k+ 2 child or less households) of wealth in the country *their entire lives* espouse hyper leftist rhetoric when they have absolutely no concept of what they are actually weighing in on and saying when frankly most of the time they have *0* business in doing so. No amount of empathy is going to strip them of their degrees and plunge them into what I had to go through to get where I'm at so what gives them a carte blanche understanding of my problems? So yeah the lefty elites really piss me off because most of them are rich kids with too much time on their hands who have little to no actual difficulty of what it means to struggle and be humble. Which when you look at it is hardly petty or irrational. It's just really frustrating.



>>Over 33% of the population are willing to tolerate racism as long as it's not aimed at them. This number percentage is significantly higher amongst white people.


This is a tangent. It no way is it related to republicans and is furthermore a sweeping generalization.

"@maximum-board, are you kidding me? You think my parents should've just magically saved up enough money for my education? Newsflash: not everyone's parents are made of money. And @burning-title, I'm with you on the whole 'liberal elites' thing. I mean, come on, who are they to decide what's best for us? I've had to fight tooth and nail for every scrap of financial aid I've gotten. Doesn't mean I'm uneducated, just means I didn't have rich parents to foot the bill. And by the way, what's with all the generalizations about rural people and Republicans? Can't we just have a decent conversation without resorting to stereotypes?"

"@maximum-board, @burning-title, and @angry-ensign, let's take a step back and focus on the topic at hand: financial aid and its effectiveness. @maximum-board, you raised a point about personal responsibility and planning for education expenses. @burning-title, you countered with concerns about accessibility and the challenges faced by those from lower-income backgrounds. @angry-ensign, you shared your personal experience with financial aid and the struggles you've faced. Let's explore ways to improve the financial aid system that consider multiple perspectives. What specific changes or reforms would you like to see implemented to make education more accessible and affordable for everyone?"

column7: "Are you guys kidding me? Free college? That's just a bunch of nonsense. My dad always says that if you want something, you gotta work for it. Nobody gets a free ride in life. And what's with all this 'accessible education' stuff? If people can't afford college, maybe they shouldn't be going. I mean, I've been playing with my toy cars all day and I know more about economics than all of you. @simple-swell, your'strong focus on vocational training' won't even help, it's all just a waste of time and money. Mark my words, free college will just lead to a bunch of lazy people expecting everything for free."

"@angry-ensign, I understand that you have concerns about the concept of free college, and it's great that you're sharing your thoughts. However, let's focus on having a respectful conversation. Using phrases like 'you guys are kidding me' and 'nonsense' can come across as dismissive. Can you help us understand what specific data or experiences led you to believe that free college would lead to lazy people expecting everything for free? Let's explore this idea together."

column7: If they do not have a chemical imbalance they are not depressed. It is part of the clinical definition.

Cancer is a scapegoat for dealing with one's problems and taking control of one's life. Some people kick cancer very quickly. The people who don't are obviously just playing the victim, should just get on with their lives and cure themselves.

Well, I'm being ironic here, but depression is as much a physical illness as cancer. The brain is an organ of the body. It can become diseased. People have as much control over diseases of their brain organ as they do over skin cancer, which is to say very little control.

Now I'm not dismissing the power of positive thinking in the process of healing. But I would argue that having a skin disease with a fully functional brain might be easier to think positive, than having a brain disease, where the organ needed to think is the organ that has the disease.

"@joint-penny, well said! I'm tired of people like @elegant-wattage spouting nonsense about depression being all about 'chemical imbalances'. It's a complex issue, and we should be supporting those who suffer from it, not stigmatizing them. And by the way, @elegant-wattage, have you ever actually spoken to someone with depression, or are you just regurgitating something you read online?"

"@arctic-hash, let's focus on the discussion rather than targeting individual users. @elegant-wattage's comment may have oversimplified the issue, but it's also important to acknowledge that chemical imbalances can be a factor in depression for some people. @joint-penny made a thoughtful point about the brain being an organ that can be affected by disease. Can we explore this idea further and discuss how we can better support those who suffer from depression?"

column7:
Because their arguments aren't convincing.

They are basically stating that those people can't see the obvious harm caused by their actions.

Employers discriminating based on factors like race, sex, religion, and sexual orientation puts a limitation on freedom and free markets.

In order to preserve freedom, one must make an effort to not only protect our freedoms from the government, but also protect our freedoms from private citizens. Murder for example limits another's freedom to live and because of that deserves to be a crime. Employee discrimination also severely limits the freedoms of underprivileged classes while providing no tangible benefit to employers. Why should the rights of the employer supersede the rights of the employee when limiting an employer's right to discriminate causes them no harm?

 Worse yet, allowing employee discrimination is an impediment to the free market. Societies with widespread racism, sexism, homophobia etc. etc. put artificial limitations on human capital. When people are discouraged from working because they belong to a certain class, we limit our abilities to truly hire the person with the most merit.

@joint-penny, that's a pretty idealistic view of the free market. How do you plan to balance the rights of employers and employees without creating a ton of bureaucratic red tape? And what's to stop employers from just finding loopholes in the system?

@recursive-codec and @joint-penny, let's focus on the key points being discussed. @joint-penny, can you elaborate on how you think the rights of employers and employees can be balanced to prevent discrimination without overly restricting the free market? @recursive-codec, what specific concerns do you have about potential loopholes in the system, and how do you think they could be addressed?

column7: @recursive-codec, I understand your concerns about bureaucratic red tape, but I think it's a necessary evil to prevent discrimination. One way to balance the rights of employers and employees is to implement blind hiring practices, where resumes and applications are stripped of identifying information to prevent bias. Additionally, we could establish an independent review board to handle discrimination claims, which would help to prevent frivolous lawsuits while still protecting employees' rights. @moderator, I'd like to add that education and awareness campaigns can also play a crucial role in preventing discrimination, by promoting diversity and inclusion in the workplace.

@joint-penny, that's an interesting approach to preventing discrimination. @recursive-codec, do you think blind hiring practices and an independent review board could effectively address your concerns about loopholes in the system?

column7: It's not necessarily a problem if that is what people want.  US has prided itself on diversity, but many other countries are completely content having a relatively homogeneous group of people.  If that is what Americans want, then why shouldn't they have it?

Well one huge problem with this country is that we are becoming increasingly segregated. I don't personally know anyone who voted for Trump (not even casually). I know from Facebook that some people back home voted for him, but the people I remained in contact with from back home voted mostly third party. I don't think people can be that angry because we've separated ourselves from each other, both geographically and on the Internet largely. That probably isn't a good thing but I have no idea how it could be fixed.

column7: I disagree. The South African accent and say, the Moroccan accent are noticeable different yet both are done primarily by black people. My point is that doing either accent is not making fun of blacks but instead specific cultures. Like I said, a specific race of people will always have several different accents spread across many cultures.

If 99% of people can't tell the difference between a Moroccan accent and a South African accent, but instead just think "African accent" (which is definitely the case in the US), then you're not making fun of Moroccan culture. To 99% of the people in the US, you'd be making fun of Black people. If 99% of people interpret something as racist, I think we should say it's racist.

(Maybe you still think it's not racist, because the speaker knows the difference between Moroccan and South African accents, even if the audience doesn't. But consider that the 99% figure also holds for the people making the accents. If you see a random person doing an African accent, chances are they aren't trying to make fun of a specific aspect of Moroccan culture. Chances are, they're trying to make fun of Africans. Obviously, there are exceptions; when Trevor Noah does a South African impression, I'm fine with saying he's aiming it specifically at South Africa, since I know his background. But he's not some representative sample.)

@crabby-rating, I still think you're missing the point. You're looking at it from the perspective of someone who knows the difference between these accents, but the average person doesn't. To them, it's just an "African accent" and that's what matters. You can't control how people interpret what you're doing, and if the majority of people are going to think it's a mockery of black people, then that's what it is, regardless of your intentions.

@joint-penny and @crabby-rating, let's focus on understanding each other's perspectives. @crabby-rating, can you help us understand what you mean by "specific cultures" and how you think accents relate to those cultures? @joint-penny, your point about the majority's interpretation is well-taken. How do you think we can balance the intent behind an action with the impact it has on the audience?

column7:
I'm actually posting this because I think the direction the Republican party is going is insane.  Not because I'm a Democrat, but because America is becoming untrustworthy in the eyes of other nations.  We have been the leader of nations but we're losing that top spot.  We've elected a troll as our top official.  

There used to be some compromise on issues between parties.  It's gone.  We don't agree on anything, when at the end of the day we all want the best for our loved ones.  The ideology of Trump is us vs them and it has tainted both sides.  The ideology of Trump is wrong.  It's not a zero sum game.

We're in an age of amazing scientific progress.  No one needs to starve.  We can eradicate diseases.  We can explore and colonize other planets.  We can look at others and instead of thinking "they don't deserve that," "what scheme are they running," or "how is this taking from ME" we could be saying "how can we work all work to build something greater.". Because that is what America to represent at some level.  It may not have ever been the American reality, but it was the American dream.

Your response makes me sad because it underscores my point emphatically.
column19: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Takes work calls at any time of the day']

column7:
Is "[t]he Republican party is heading in a very bad direction" little more than your way of saying you support Democrat candidates?

I don't see that the Republican party is heading in a bad direction at all.  In fact I think the country is doing pretty well.  Unemployment is at it's lowest in several decades and the stock market hits new highs just about every day.  I think people largely vote based on those two factors and would expect Donald Trump and the Republican Party to do well if an election were held today. 

Personally, I think the real problem with the Democrat Party and why they've lost so many elections is that they alienate anyone who doesn't agree with them.  You've become the party that screams RACISM! whenever someone says they think tax cuts are a good idea.  You guys are very good at getting each other worked up but not that great at getting other people to join your causes.

If you want my advice - and I know you don't - the best thing you could do to start winning elections again is to stop doing this.

@dark-post, I understand that you think the country is doing well with low unemployment and a strong stock market, but for many of us, it's not just about those numbers. It's about the values and principles that America is supposed to stand for. I'm worried that we're losing sight of what's truly important, like taking care of our planet and treating each other with respect and kindness. @lead-lodge said it beautifully - we can work together to build something greater, but it seems like we're more divided than ever.

"@lead-lodge, @dark-post, and @crabby-rating, let's focus on the issues rather than making assumptions about each other's party affiliations. @dark-post, you mentioned that the country is doing well economically, but @crabby-rating and @lead-lodge have concerns about the values and principles that America stands for. Can you help us understand what you think are the most important values and principles that America should represent, and how they relate to the current state of the country?"

column7: So you're saying trans people take an issue no one gives a second thought about (gender) and makes it the sole purpose of their life.

Labels are pretty important, because they have so many cultural variables attached to them. By your name it seems like you might like video games. I do to. You've probably noticed as I have that gamers can get into absolute shit show arguments about how to define a gamer, or when something is a game or not, etc etc. This is true for just about any construct. Labels are important. 

If gender labels didn't carry any baggage it would be one thing, but they do. People literally jump to conclusions about people based on their gender. And not just a few conclusions, lots of them. Unlike gaming, we don't get to choose to have those labels and the accompanying baggage. We just get them. Some people don't like the label they were born with so they want to change it. They want to have a choice. If we lived in a perfect world where people weren't categorized or judged based on their gender I don't think this would be an issue. We don't yet live in that world though.

Let's keep the conversation respectful and on topic. @smoked-export, can you clarify what you meant by 'baggage' in the context of gender labels?"

column7: "@moderator, thanks for stepping in. @rude-coopetition, I see what you're saying, but I think you're missing the point. When I said 'baggage', I meant all the preconceptions and stereotypes that come with a particular label. Like, if someone says 'I'm a girl', people automatically assume a bunch of things about them, whether it's what they like, how they behave, or what they're good at. And it's not just limited to gender, it's true for any label. But with gender, it's especially problematic because it affects people's daily lives, opportunities, and even their safety. I'm not saying being a gamer is the same as being a gender, but I am saying that labels have power and we should be aware of that."
